blocks.content: By eight-thirty, Ethan had showered, changed, and made it back to The Copper Mug with his sketchbook tucked beneath one arm.
Mr. Alvarez had already unlocked the front door.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: You are late,
the older man said from behind the counter.
Ethan glanced at the clock.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: You opened three minutes ago.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: Late.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I ran farther today.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: Excuses are free. Coffee is not.
Ethan smiled despite himself.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Morning to you too.

Mr. Alvarez was somewhere in his sixties, though he refused to confirm any number above forty-five. He had silver hair, thick glasses, and the kind of face that made people confess things they had only come in to caffeinate through.
He placed Ethan’s usual cup on the counter before Ethan ordered.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: Two sugars,
he said.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: Because apparently bitterness should only exist in art.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: That’s actually a good line.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: You may paint it on my wall for half price.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I’ll consider being exploited.

blocks.speaker: Mr. Alvarez
blocks.content: That is the spirit of capitalism.
Ethan paid and took his coffee to the corner table near the window.
His table.
Not officially, of course. But unofficially enough that other regulars avoided it when he was there.
He set his sketchbook down, opened to a blank page, and tapped his pencil once against the paper.
A blank page never scared him.
People did.
Blank pages were honest. They didn’t pretend to be simple when they weren’t. They didn’t expect him to know the right thing to say. They didn’t look disappointed when he took too long to answer.
He stared out the window.
Town was fully awake now. Delivery trucks grumbled past. A woman carried flowers wrapped in brown paper. Two kids argued outside the bakery over who got the bigger pastry.
Ethan began sketching the street without thinking too hard.
That was usually when his best work happened.
When his mind stepped aside.
A line for the curb. A soft angle for the café window. The bookstore sign. The bakery awning. People as motion first, detail second.
Then red hair.
His pencil paused.

Across the street, the girl from earlier walked beside another girl with dark curly hair. They were laughing now, shoulders nearly bumping, both carrying drinks. The red-haired girl had a stuffed purple dinosaur tucked under one arm like a trophy.
Ethan blinked.
She had actually won something from that arcade machine.
That alone felt impossible.
She turned suddenly, walking backward as she spoke to her friend, animated hands cutting shapes into the air. The dinosaur nearly slipped. She caught it against her chest and laughed again.
People looked at her.
Not in a strange way.
In the way people looked at something bright passing through a room.
Ethan looked down at his page.
Without meaning to, he had drawn her outline into the street scene.
Not her face. Not fully.
Just movement.
The tilt of her shoulders. The wild fall of her hair. The dinosaur under her arm.
He frowned.
Great. That’s normal. Sketch random strangers like a total creep.
He flipped the page quickly, though not before noticing Mr. Alvarez watching him from behind the counter.
Ethan cleared his throat and pretended to be very invested in his coffee.
Mr. Alvarez lifted one eyebrow.
Ethan ignored him.
Or tried to.
The bell above the café door jingled.
For one panicked second, Ethan thought she had come inside.
She hadn’t.
It was only an elderly man in a brown coat.
Ethan exhaled slowly.
Relax. You saw a pretty girl. Civilization will recover.
Still, he found himself glancing back through the window.
She was gone.
And the street looked ordinary again.
